elements.sent-2411-001: A fundamental question is understanding the rate at which random quantum circuits converge to the Haar measure.
elements.sent-2411-002: One quantity which is important in establishing this rate is the spectral gap of a random quantum ensemble.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this work we establish a new bound on the spectral gap of the $t$-th moment of a one-dimensional brickwork architecture on $N$ qudits.
elements.sent-2411-004: This bound is independent of both $t$ and $N$, provided $t$ does not exceed the qudit dimension $q$.
elements.sent-2411-005: We also show that the bound is nearly optimal.
elements.sent-2411-006: The improved spectral gaps gives large improvements to the constant factors in known results on the approximate $t$-design depths of the 1D brickwork, of generic circuit architectures, and of specially-constructed architectures which scramble in depth $O(\log N)$.
elements.sent-2411-007: We moreover show that the spectral gap gives the dominant $\epsilon$-dependence of the t-design depth at small-$\epsilon$.
elements.sent-2411-008: Our spectral gap bound is obtained by bounding the $N$-site 1D brickwork architecture by the spectra of 3-site operators.
elements.sent-2411-009: We then exploit a block-triangular hierarchy and a global symmetry in these operators in order to efficiently bound them.
elements.sent-2411-010: The technical methods used are a qualitatively different approach for bounding spectral gaps and and have little in common with previous techniques.
elements.sent-2411-011: Random quantum circuits are an important model in quantum information theory and beyond.
elements.sent-2411-012: They are used in quantum advantage experiments \cite{Boixo2018, Arute2019,Movassagh2019 , as a representation of unstructured dynamics in physical systems like black holes \cite{Brown2018,Hayden2007 , and as a setting for studying measurement-induced phase transitions \cite{Skinner2019,Bao2020 as well as the AdS/CFT correspondence \cite{Jafferis2022 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Central to most of these applications is the approximate $t$-design depth, which characterizes the depth at which the moments of a random quantum circuit ensemble become difficult to distinguish from those of the Haar measure.
elements.sent-2411-014: Many of the known results on $t$-design depths are based on the spectral gap (see Definition~\ref{def:spectral_gap ) of the 1D brickwork architecture.}
elements.sent-2411-015: Refs.~\onlinecite{Brandao2016,Haferkamp2022,Chen2024b use bounds on the spectral gap to bound the additive-error and multiplicative-error approximate t-design depths of the 1D brickwork architecture.}
elements.sent-2411-016: These bounds scale as $O(N)$, where $N$ is the number of sites.
elements.sent-2411-017: Refs.~\onlinecite{Schuster2024,LaRacuente2024 , meanwhile, use results on the spectral gap of the brickwork to construct families of circuits that form approximate $t$-designs in depth $O(\log N)$.}
elements.sent-2411-018: Ref.~\onlinecite{Belkin2023 gives a reduction from spectral gaps of arbitrary architectures to that of the 1D brickwork to show that all well-connected circuit architectures form approximate $t$-designs in depth $\text{poly (N)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-019: In this work we give improved bounds on the spectral gap of the 1D brickwork when the qudit dimension $q$ exceeds $t$.
elements.sent-2411-020: We further show that our bounds on the spectral gap are nearly tight, providing a matching lower bound which differs from our upper bound by at most 15\%.
elements.sent-2411-021: We also show that the spectral gap is the sole quantity controlling the asymptotic small-$\epsilon$ scaling of the $\epsilon$-approximate $t$-design depth.
elements.sent-2411-022: More precisely we provide both upper and lower bounds on the depth of the form $\log\left(\frac{1 {\sqrt{1-\Delta }\right)^{-1}\log\left(\frac{1 {\epsilon}\right) + o\left[\log\left(\frac{1 {\epsilon}\right)\right]$ , where $\Delta$ is the spectral gap.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-023: It follows that our upper and lower bounds on the spectral gap give upper and lower bounds on the approximate $t$-design depth, and that the true formula for the approximate $t$-design depth must involve the spectral gap.
elements.sent-2411-024: Our spectral gap bound also improves the approximate $t-$design depth for each of the five bounds listed above.
elements.sent-2411-025: The asymptotic $N$-dependencies do not change, but the constant factors often change dramatically.
elements.sent-2411-026: We show, for example, that the 100-site 1D brickwork of local dimension $4$ forms a $10^{-4}$-approximate $4$-design in at most 3030 layers.
elements.sent-2411-027: The best previously-known bound for this case was $1.77 \cdot 10^{15}$ layers\cite{Brandao2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-028: Our bound does not vary with $t$, but it is valid only when the local Hilbert space dimension $q \geq t$.
elements.sent-2411-029: If our proof technique can be extended to $q < t$, the asymptotic $t$-dependence of these bounds will also improve by a factor of $\text{polylog (t)$.}
elements.sent-2411-030: The first step of our proof is a reduction from the $N$-site brickwork to a series of $3$-site operators.
elements.sent-2411-031: A bound on the spectral gaps of the $3$-site operators implies a bound on the spectral gap of the full operator.
elements.sent-2411-032: Moreover, this relationship between the spectral gap bounds is independent of $N,q,$ and $t$.
elements.sent-2411-033: We then show that the spectral gaps of the $3$-site operators do not depend on $t$ so long as $t \leq q$.
elements.sent-2411-034: The restriction on $t$ enters into our proof quite late, and so it is possible that many of the techniques we develop will be useful for establishing the spectral gap in the $t > q$ case.
elements.sent-2411-035: Some of the mathematical structure we develop is also relevant to non-brickwork architectures or properties of the random circuit other than the spectral gap, such as anticoncentration~\cite{Dalzell2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-036: Our proof exploits three types of structure in the eigenspaces of the moment operator.
elements.sent-2411-037: We first describe a block-triangular structure due to the 1D geometry of the circuit.
elements.sent-2411-038: This is similar in flavor to the known $t = 2$ block-triangular structure exploited by Ref.~\cite{Deneris2024 , but not equivalent even in the $t = 2$ case.}
elements.sent-2411-039: Using this structure we reduce the problem of bounding the $N$-site brickwork to that of bounding a family of $3$-site circuits.
elements.sent-2411-040: Second, we develop a block-triangular structure from the subgroup structure of the permutation group.
elements.sent-2411-041: This allows us to decompose the eigenspaces of the $t$\textsuperscript{th moment operator, isolating new eigenvalues from those inherited from the $(t-1)$\textsuperscript{th moment operator.}}
elements.sent-2411-042: Third, we find a block-diagonal structure which corresponds to the irreducible representations of the symmetric group.
elements.sent-2411-043: We believe this proof technique is independently interesting beyond our new bounds on the spectral gap.
elements.sent-2411-044: It has very little in common with prior methods, such as detectability lemma-based approaches that bound the spectral gap through the Nachtergaele method\cite{Brandao2016 , Knabe bounds\cite{Haferkamp2021 , or relating to the PFC ensemble\cite{Chen2024b .}}}

elements.subsection-2411-014: Obtaining the approximate $t$-design depth from the spectral gap
elements.sent-2411-388: In order to obtain a bound on the approximate $t$-design depth from an eigenvalue gap in the transfer matrix, we use the following:.
elements.sent-2411-389: The $N$-site 1D brickwork architecture with local Hilbert space dimension $q$ forms an additive-error $\epsilon$-approximate $t$-design after at most \begin{equation}1 + \frac{2}{\log \frac{1}{\estair(N,q,t)}}\left(2 N t \log q + \log \frac{1}{\epsilon}\right)\end{equation} layers and a multiplicative-error\footnote{The multiplicative-error $t$-design condition, as can be seen in Ref.~\onlinecite{Brandao2016, Chen2024b, Schuster2024 , requires $(1-\epsilon)\Psi_{\text{Haar \preceq \Psi_{\varepsilon \preceq (1+\epsilon) \Psi_{\text{Haar }$, where $\Psi_{\nu}$ is the operator formed by taking the Choi-Jamiolkowski isomorphism of $\Phi_\nu$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-390: Here $\estair(N,q,t)$ is the magnitude of the largest eigenvalue of \(\Tstair\).
elements.sent-2411-391: We first prove the additive-error bound.
elements.sent-2411-392: Combining the bounds of Lemmas \ref{lemma:frame_potential_from_eigenvalue , \ref{lemma:hermitianized_brickwork_is_staircase , and \ref{lemma:diamond_norm_to_frame_potential , we see \begin{equation}||\Phi_\varepsilon - \Phi_Haar||_\diamond^2 \leq q^{2Nt} \left(q^{2Nt}|\estair|^{\ell - 1}\right)\end{equation} The diamond-norm distance is guaranteed to be below \(\epsilon\) so long as \begin{equation}\epsilon \geq q^{2Nt}\estair^\frac{\ell - 1}{2}\end{equation} or equivalently \begin{equation}\log \frac{1}{\epsilon} \leq -2 N t \log q + \frac{\ell - 1}{2} \log \frac{1}{\estair}\end{equation} Solving for \(\ell\) gives the desired result.}}}
elements.sent-2411-393: For the multiplicative-error bound, we can use Lemma 4 of Ref.~\onlinecite{Brandao2016 to see that the 1D brickwork forms a multiplicative-error $\epsilon$-approximate $t$-design when \begin{equation}\epsilon \geq q^{2Nt}\lambda(\ell,N,q,t)\end{equation} We then apply Lemmas \ref{lemma:brickwork_power_gap and \ref{lemma:hermitianized_brickwork_is_staircase to write this as \begin{equation}\epsilon \geq q^{2Nt}\estair^{\lfloor \ell/2 \rfloor}\end{equation} and rearrange to obtain \begin{equation}\ell \geq 2\left\lceil \frac{1}{2\log \frac{1}{\estair}} \left(2Nt\log q + \log \frac{1}{\epsilon}\right) \right\rceil\end{equation}.}}}

elements.sent-2411-548: We start with Lemma $\ref{lemma:derangement_polynomial_productform}$, which we express as \begin{equation}d_t(x) = c_t(x^2) (-1)^t \sum_{k=0}^t (-1)^{t-k} \binom{t}{k} \prod_{\ell = 0}^{k-1} (1+g_\ell(x)) \label{eq:dt_sum}\end{equation} Note that $g_\ell(0) = 0$.
elements.sent-2411-549: Ignoring the prefactor $c_t(x^2)$ for now, this expression can be described as a weighted sum over paths.
elements.sent-2411-550: Each path occurs over $t$ total time steps.
elements.sent-2411-551: At each time step we either move forward a space, acquiring weight $\frac{(1 + \ell x)^2 {1 + \ell x^2 = 1+g_\ell(x)$ if we are originally at position $\ell$, or we do not advance, acquiring a weight of $-1$.}}
elements.sent-2411-552: Any path that advances $k$ steps in total therefore acquires a weight $(-1)^{t-k \prod_{\ell = 0 ^{k-1 (1+g_\ell(x))$, and there are $\binom{t {k}$ such paths.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-553: At the end of this process, we sum over all weights of all possible paths, regardless of the final position.
elements.sent-2411-554: This weighted path sum can therefore be represented as a matrix element \begin{equation}u^T M^t \hat{e}_0\end{equation} where $M$ is the transfer matrix representing all possible actions at a single time step.
elements.sent-2411-555: The decision to advance and acquire a weight $(1+g_\ell(x))$ corresponds to the transfer matrix $T + TG(x)$, while the decision to remain still and acquire a weight $(-1)$ corresponds to $-I$.
elements.sent-2411-556: Therefore $M=T+TG-I$, so \begin{equation}d_t(x) = c_t(x^2) u^T \big[T + TG(x) - I\big]^t \hat{e}_0 = c_t(x^2) u^T \big[\Delta + TG\big]^t \hat{e}_0\end{equation}-.

elements.sent-2411-557: Define an operator $\delta$ which maps diagonal matrices to diagonal matrices by \begin{equation}\delta M = \sum_{i} (M_{i,i} - M_{i-1,i-1})e_i e_i^T\end{equation} We have the following bound on the derangement polynomial: \begin{equation}d_t(x) \leq c_t(x^2)\sum_A \binom{t}{A} A^{t-A} \max_{\lambda} \left| u^T \prod_{i=1}^{A} T^{\lambda_i+1} \delta^{\lambda_i} G(x) \hat{e}_0\right|. \label{eq:dt_inequality}\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-563: This lets us impose the inequality \begin{equation}\label{eq:delta_inequality} \big|\hat{e}_m^T\Delta\left(\prod_{i=0}^\ell T^{k_i} D_i\right) \hat{e}_0\big| \leq \ell \max_j \big|\hat{e}_m^T\prod_{i=0}^\ell\left[(1-\delta_{ij})T^{k_i} D_i + \delta_{ij}T^{k_i+1} \delta D_i \right] \hat{e}_0\big|\end{equation} Now we generalize the situation to a sequence of $t$ operators which could either be $\Delta$ or $TG$.
